title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardianship of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Mahi Kaitiaki: Te Tiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: Students will explore the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (our local stream) through the lens of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) and connect to the pūrākau (legend) of Kahungunu and the tuna (eel) that sustained our tūpuna. The lesson will examine how the stream's health directly impacts the physical, mental, and spiritual wellbeing of our Ngāti Kahungunu community.
learningObjectives: Analyze water quality data from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to assess its impact on community physical health and mahinga kai (food gathering) practices.
Explain the concept of kaitiakitanga and its connection to holistic wellbeing (hauora) using examples from local Ngāti Kahungunu knowledge and practices.
Collaboratively design and propose a practical kaitiaki action plan to address an identified environmental issue affecting the awa and community wellbeing.
Reflect on personal and collective responsibility as kaitiaki in maintaining the mauri (life force) of our local environment.

activities.title: Whakatau & Whakawhanaungatanga: Connecting to the Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge the mauri of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students share their own connections and experiences with the awa. The teacher shares the pūrākau of Kahungunu and the significance of the tuna, linking cultural history to environmental and community health.

activities.title: He Rangahau Hauora o te Awa: Stream Health Investigation
activities.description: At a designated site on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, students work in small groups to conduct practical water quality tests (pH, nitrate, turbidity, macroinvertebrate survey) using testing kits. They record data and make initial observations on the health of the awa and its capacity to support life and wellbeing.

activities.title: Kōrero with a Kaitiaki: Community Expert Session
activities.description: A local kaumātua or representative from Manaaki Te Awa Trust visits the class. They speak on the historical and current health of the awa, how its decline impacts cultural practices and community hauora, and what active kaitiakitanga looks like for Ngāti Kahungunu.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Hōu: Designing a Kaitiaki Action Plan
activities.description: Using their collected data and insights from the kaitiaki, groups brainstorm a specific, actionable project to improve the health of the awa (e.g., planting day, community education campaign, riparian planting proposal, monitoring programme). Plans must consider environmental, social, and cultural outcomes for wellbeing.

activities.title: Whakapuaki: Presenting and Reflecting
activities.description: Groups present their Kaitiaki Action Plans to the class and invited community members for feedback. This is followed by a individual reflective writing task, connecting the learning journey back to the dimensions of their own hauora (te whare tapa whā).

resources: Laurence Smith (Kaumātua, Ngāti Kahungunu) or representative from Manaaki Te Awa Trust
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'Wai Quality' monitoring kits and data sheets
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) online resources
Digital devices for accessing local historical photos and current environmental data on the Hawke's Bay Regional Council portal

tasks: Completed stream health investigation data sheet with analysis.
Group presentation of a 'Kaitiaki Action Plan' proposal.
Individual written reflection on personal role as a kaitiaki and connection to hauora.

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; B3 - Interpersonal skills; C3 - Community resources; D3 - Societal attitudes and values
Health and Physical Education: Strand A - Personal Health and Physical Development; Strand C - Relationships with Other People; Strand D - Healthy Communities and Environments
Science: Nature of Science - Participating and Contributing; Living World - Ecology
Social Studies: Continuity and Change; Place and Environment
